title: Symptom Control in Patients with Cancer
authors: Charles L. Loprinzi, Thomas J. Smith

items.title: Why systematic patient-reported symptom monitoring improves survival
items.content:
Prompt symptom assessment is not just supportive care — it changes outcomes.
Randomized trials show that for metastatic cancer patients, prompt symptom assessment and resolution lead to substantially increased survival.
In Basch's trial of 766 metastatic solid-tumor patients, weekly patient-reported-outcome (PRO) monitoring with nurse follow-up of symptom 'spikes' improved quality of life and raised median overall survival to 31.2 months versus 26 months with usual care (p = .03).
This benefit may also explain why patients randomized to concurrent palliative care alongside cancer care live longer.
keyPoints: Weekly PRO symptom monitoring improved median OS (31.2 vs 26 months, p=.03)
Nurses act on symptom 'spikes' (e.g., pain) and bring patients in
Concurrent palliative care is associated with longer survival
items.reference: THE FIRST STEP: "median overall survival (OS) was 31.2 months in the PRO group and 26 months in the usual care group (p = .03)."

items.title: Why a structured checklist captures far more symptoms than open questioning
items.content:
How you ask determines what you find.
Homsi et al. evaluated 200 patients with a 48-item symptom checklist versus what patients spontaneously volunteered.
The median number of volunteered symptoms was one; the median number using a checklist was ten.
Free, validated scales such as the Edmonton Symptom Assessment System–Revised take an average of only 117 seconds to complete, so time is not a barrier — and the first step is to establish the symptom's generators and classify it into a treatable subtype rather than just asking for a 0–10 number.
keyPoints: Checklist captures ~10 symptoms vs ~1 volunteered
ESAS-R is free, multilingual, and ~117 s to complete
Classify the symptom into a treatable subtype before reflexively scoring 0–10
items.reference: THE FIRST STEP: "The median number of volunteered symptoms was one; the median number using a checklist was 10."

items.title: How to evaluate and treat cancer-related fatigue
items.content:
Fatigue is the most common cancer symptom (often ≥80% prevalence) and frequently has reversible contributors.
Causes include the cancer itself, electrolyte abnormalities (especially hypercalcemia), anemia, hypothyroidism, chemotherapy, and immunotherapy (checkpoint inhibitors, which cause hypothyroidism in 5–22% and de novo hypophysitis in 1–2%).
Assessment should check for hypothyroidism, anemia, hypercalcemia and other electrolytes, and renal failure, and screen for depression/demoralization — noting that immunotherapy-induced hypothyroidism can appear months after the drug is stopped.
Treatment starts with correcting anemia, hypothyroidism, and electrolytes; structured exercise is preventive and therapeutic, and trials of methylphenidate, modafinil, or bupropion can help.
keyPoints: Fatigue is the commonest symptom (≥80%); look for reversible causes
Causes: hypercalcemia, anemia, hypothyroidism (incl. checkpoint-inhibitor 5–22%, can appear months later), depression
Treat reversibles; structured exercise; consider methylphenidate, modafinil, or bupropion
items.reference: FATIGUE (Treatment): "Paradoxically, structured exercise (e.g., 45 min of walking daily) has been shown to be preventative and therapeutic."

items.title: How to escalate analgesia for nociceptive cancer pain
items.content:
Nociceptive pain follows a stepwise analgesic ladder with mandatory bowel care.
Start with acetaminophen, then nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory agents, and then opioids — which should always be used with constipation-preventing measures.
Notably, recent studies show that adding acetaminophen to high doses of opioids has no additional effect.
Steroids slightly improve cancer pain versus placebo (a Cochrane review found ~0.84-point reduction on a 0–10 scale at 1 week) and also improve fatigue, nausea, and well-being.
keyPoints: Ladder: acetaminophen → NSAIDs → opioids (always with constipation prophylaxis)
Adding acetaminophen to high-dose opioids gives no extra benefit
Steroids modestly reduce pain (~0.84/10 at 1 week) plus help fatigue/nausea/well-being
items.reference: NOCICEPTIVE PAIN: "start with acetaminophen, then nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory agents, and then opioids, which should always be used with constipation-preventing measures."

items.title: Why opioids are useful for neuropathic cancer pain despite common teaching
items.content:
A widely taught belief — that opioids do not work for neuropathic pain — is contradicted by trials.
Randomized trials show that opioids do help neuropathic pain with as much benefit as gabapentin or nortriptyline, and the combination is even more effective.
Many agents are used (gabapentin, pregabalin, duloxetine, nortriptyline, amitriptyline, carbamazepine, lamotrigine), but the number needed to treat ranges from 3 to 7 or even 10, so trials of alternate drugs are needed.
Pain relief, if it occurs, appears within 4–8 days, so individual drug trials need not exceed 2–3 weeks.
keyPoints: Opioids help neuropathic pain as much as gabapentin/nortriptyline; combinations are better
High NNT (3–10) means sequential drug trials are often required
Relief occurs within 4–8 days — trials need only ~2–3 weeks
items.reference: NEUROPATHIC PAIN: "Contrary to common teaching, randomized trials show that opioids do help neuropathic pain with as much benefit as gabapentin or nortriptyline; the combination is even more effective."

items.title: How to manage chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy
items.content:
CIPN affects the majority of patients receiving neurotoxic chemotherapy and has limited preventive options.
The biggest offenders are taxanes, platinums, epothilones, eribulin, vinca alkaloids, bortezomib, and lenalidomide.
There are no clearly established means of preventing CIPN other than decreasing the dose or stopping the offending drug; ASCO guidelines suggest considering dose attenuation or stopping the drug in patients who develop substantial CIPN during treatment.
For established CIPN after completing neurotoxic chemotherapy, duloxetine can attenuate symptoms to a small degree (0.6–1.0 on a 0–10 scale) and is currently the only proven treatment.
keyPoints: Offenders: taxanes, platinums, epothilones, eribulin, vinca alkaloids, bortezomib, lenalidomide
Prevention: only dose reduction or stopping the drug (cryotherapy/compression are investigational)
Established CIPN: duloxetine gives a small benefit (0.6–1.0/10) and is the only proven treatment
items.reference: CIPN: "there is evidence from more than one clinical trial that supports that duloxetine can attenuate established neuropathy symptoms to a small degree (0.6–1.0 on a 0–10 scale)."

items.title: Why octreotide is useful in malignant bowel obstruction
items.content:
Visceral pain is among the hardest pains to treat.
Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory agents or neuropathic-pain medications such as gabapentin or pregabalin, combined with opioids, may be required.
Octreotide in combination with opioids reduces visceral hyperalgesia and may be helpful if nothing else works for malignant bowel obstruction.
This reflects octreotide's reduction of GI secretions and distension that drive visceral pain.
keyPoints: Visceral pain is among the hardest to treat
Combine opioids with NSAIDs or gabapentin/pregabalin
Octreotide + opioids reduces visceral hyperalgesia in refractory malignant bowel obstruction
items.reference: VISCERAL PAIN: "Octreotide in combination with opioids reduces visceral hyperalgesia and may be helpful if nothing else works for malignant bowel obstruction."

items.title: How oral cryotherapy prevents fluorouracil-induced mucositis
items.content:
Mucositis is largely a timing- and drug-specific diagnosis with a simple preventive measure for one regimen.
Oral mucositis occurs in 30–40% of people on molecularly targeted agents (regorafenib, sorafenib, erdafitinib, palbociclib); among commonly used chemotherapy drugs, doxorubicin is the most common cause.
Most mucositis appears 7–10 days after treatment, and GI mucositis (diarrhea) occurs in 50–80%.
For patients receiving bolus fluorouracil, sucking on ice chips during the infusion (oral cryotherapy) reduces incidence and severity by about 50% — vasoconstriction limits drug delivery to the oral mucosa.
keyPoints: Oral mucositis: 30–40% with targeted agents; doxorubicin commonest among standard chemo
Onset typically 7–10 days post-treatment; GI mucositis (diarrhea) in 50–80%
Bolus 5-FU: oral cryotherapy (ice chips during infusion) cuts mucositis ~50%
items.reference: Treatment (Mucositis): "For patients receiving bolus fluorouracil, sucking on ice chips during infusion (oral cryotherapy) reduces the incidence and severity by ~50%."

items.title: How to treat chemotherapy-induced diarrhea from GI mucositis
items.content:
GI mucositis can denude the gut and cause high-output diarrhea.
At its worst, the entire GI tract can be denuded and cannot reabsorb the ~25 L of intestinal fluid made daily, producing high-output diarrhea (liters per day).
Loperamide is the first choice, up to 24 mg a day.
Octreotide up to 300 mg a day is reserved for refractory cases, and glucocorticoids are also commonly employed; if the time course is wrong (very soon or weeks after therapy), other causes such as infection or excess laxatives should be sought.
keyPoints: Severe GI mucositis → high-output diarrhea (failure to reabsorb ~25 L/day)
Loperamide first-line, up to 24 mg/day; octreotide up to 300 mg/day for refractory cases
Wrong time course → consider infection or laxative excess
items.reference: Treatment (GI mucositis): "For GI mucositis manifested as diarrhea, loperamide is the first choice, up to 24 mg a day. Octreotide up to 300 mg a day is reserved for refractory cases."

items.title: How antiemetic therapy evolved from dexamethasone to olanzapine
items.content:
Chemotherapy-induced nausea/vomiting risk is agent-specific (cisplatin and doxorubicin are highly emetogenic; fluorouracil is not).
Antiemetic classes were established in successive decades: dexamethasone (1970s), 5-HT3 receptor antagonists (1990s), and NK1 receptor antagonists (2000s).
Because NK1 antagonists did not reduce nausea/vomiting in the first 24 hours as 5-HT3 antagonists did, they were added to — not used in place of — 5-HT3 antagonists.
In the 2010s olanzapine was shown to further decrease nausea/vomiting when added to these three classes and appears to be the most effective agent.
keyPoints: Emetogenicity is drug-specific (cisplatin/doxorubicin high; 5-FU low)
Sequence: dexamethasone → 5-HT3 → NK1 (added, not replacing 5-HT3) → olanzapine
Olanzapine is the most effective agent in the cocktail
items.reference: Treatment (Chemotherapy N/V): "olanzapine was demonstrated to decrease nausea and vomiting when added to the three drug classes discussed above. Olanzapine appears to be the most effective of all of the drugs mentioned in this section."

items.title: Why low-dose olanzapine helps nausea unrelated to chemotherapy
items.content:
Nausea/vomiting in cancer patients not on chemotherapy has many causes and few effective drugs.
Etiologies include radiation, bowel obstruction, other medications, electrolyte abnormalities, post-anesthesia state, and cerebral metastases, and traditional agents (metoclopramide, prochlorperazine, haloperidol, ondansetron, dexamethasone, dronabinol) are not very effective.
A 2020 placebo-controlled trial showed low-dose olanzapine (5 mg/d) was very effective in advanced-cancer patients who had not received chemotherapy or radiation for ≥2 weeks, dropping nausea scores from 9/10 to 2/10 the next day.
A 2023 trial showed even 2.5 mg/d markedly reduced nausea and vomiting.
keyPoints: Non-chemo nausea: radiation, obstruction, drugs, electrolytes, cerebral metastases
Traditional antiemetics are not very effective in this setting
Low-dose olanzapine (2.5–5 mg/d) is highly effective (9/10 → 2/10)
items.reference: NAUSEA AND VOMITING UNRELATED TO CHEMOTHERAPY: "a relatively low dose of olanzapine (5 mg/d) was very effective for decreasing nausea and vomiting in patients with advanced cancer who had not received chemotherapy or radiation therapy for at least 2 weeks prior..."

items.title: Why cancer cachexia cannot be reversed simply by replacing calories
items.content:
Cachexia is a metabolic syndrome, not just undernutrition.
It is multifactorial, characterized by loss of skeletal muscle and adipose tissue, an imbalance in metabolic regulation, and reduced food intake, and is strongly associated with up to 20% of all cancer deaths.
In general, cancer cachexia cannot be reversed by simply replacing calories and nutrients, and enteral feeding tubes and intravenous nutrition are not recommended and should not be used routinely.
The novel agent ponsegromab blocks elevated GDF-15 and improved appetite, weight, and activity in a phase 2 trial, though it is not FDA-approved.
keyPoints: Cachexia: muscle + fat loss with metabolic dysregulation (linked to ~20% of cancer deaths)
Cannot be reversed by replacing calories; routine enteral/IV nutrition not recommended
Ponsegromab (anti-GDF-15) improved appetite/weight/activity in phase 2 (not FDA-approved)
items.reference: ANOREXIA AND CACHEXIA (Etiology): "In general, cancer cachexia cannot be reversed by simply replacing calories and nutrients."

items.title: How to choose pharmacotherapy for cancer-related anorexia
items.content:
Drug choice for anorexia is now evidence-based, favoring one agent.
For anorexia, olanzapine is recommended as the first-line agent and the only agent with good evidence of benefit without potentially serious harm — in the largest RCT, 60% of olanzapine patients gained ≥5% weight versus 9% on placebo (p < .001).
Mirtazapine, although commonly prescribed, was ineffective in a large randomized trial and should not be used.
Megestrol acetate and corticosteroids increase appetite and cause some weight gain but have untoward side effects and do not improve survival.
keyPoints: Olanzapine is first-line for anorexia (≥5% weight gain in 60% vs 9% placebo)
Mirtazapine is ineffective — do not use
Megestrol/steroids raise appetite but have side effects and no survival benefit
items.reference: ANOREXIA AND CACHEXIA (Treatment): "For anorexia, olanzapine is recommended as the first-line agent and the only agent with good evidence of benefit without potentially serious harm."

items.title: Why every opioid prescription needs a constipation-prevention plan
items.content:
Constipation is common (20–90% of cancer patients) and largely preventable.
Causes include opioids, acetaminophen, ondansetron, some chemotherapy, and noncancer drugs, plus inactivity, dehydration, low-fiber diet, hypercalcemia, and hypothyroidism.
Every opioid prescription should be accompanied by a plan to prevent constipation.
Senna is the first choice (1–2 tablets up to 8) with adequate hydration, polyethylene glycol is added next, and opioid-refractory patients usually respond to peripherally acting opioid antagonists that reverse the opioid in the gut but not the brain.
keyPoints: Constipation occurs in 20–90%; opioids are a major cause
Pair every opioid prescription with a bowel regimen
Senna first → add PEG → peripherally acting opioid antagonist for refractory opioid constipation
items.reference: CONSTIPATION (Treatment): "Every opioid prescription should be accompanied by a plan to prevent constipation. Senna is the first choice..."

items.title: How to assess and palliate dyspnea in advanced cancer
items.content:
Dyspnea is a subjective, ominous symptom requiring systematic assessment.
It is reported by 10–70% of patients, especially toward end of life, and portends a poor prognosis (average survival of days, weeks, or months), so it should trigger serious-illness conversations and advance directives; ASCO recommends systematic assessment at every visit in advanced cancer.
First seek reversible causes (hypoxia, pneumonia, pulmonary embolism, pleural effusion, COPD, asthma, bronchial constriction) and review medications (checkpoint-inhibitor pneumonitis can appear months after stopping).
Palliation includes treating the cancer if feasible, low-dose opioids for air hunger, low-dose benzodiazepines for anxiety, a fan blowing cool air across the face, and supplemental oxygen for hypoxemia (SpO2 ≤90%).
keyPoints: Dyspnea (10–70%) signals poor prognosis → trigger serious-illness conversation/advance directive
Find reversible causes; checkpoint-inhibitor pneumonitis can appear months after stopping
Palliate with low-dose opioids (air hunger), benzodiazepines (anxiety), a fan, and O2 if SpO2 ≤90%
items.reference: DYSPNEA (Treatment): "Opioids at low doses are generally safe and can be helpful in relieving air hunger, and low-dose benzodiazepines can help relieve anxiety. A fan blowing cold air across the face can give some comfort."

items.title: How to treat hot flashes without estrogen or testosterone replacement
items.content:
Hormone-deprivation hot flashes are common and can persist for decades.
They occur in about two-thirds of postmenopausal women treated for breast cancer and three-quarters of men after orchiectomy for prostate cancer; the proximate cause is rapid loss of estrogen or testosterone.
In women, antidepressants such as venlafaxine, paroxetine, sertraline, and fluoxetine reduce hot flashes in randomized trials; the chapter authors prefer citalopram 20 mg/d, with gabapentin and oxybutynin as alternatives, and fezolinetant (an NK3 receptor antagonist, available 2023) as a newer nonhormonal option.
In men, gabapentin 900 mg/d works well, whereas a randomized trial of venlafaxine did not show significant benefit.
keyPoints: Hot flashes: ~2/3 of breast-cancer women, ~3/4 of orchiectomy men; can last decades
Women: SSRIs/SNRIs (preferred citalopram 20 mg/d), gabapentin, oxybutynin, fezolinetant (NK3 antagonist)
Men: gabapentin 900 mg/d effective; venlafaxine RCT was negative
items.reference: Treatment FEMALES (Hot Flashes): "recommend citalopram, 20 mg/d, as it appears as effective as other antidepressants and is well tolerated."

items.title: Why corticosteroids are a useful adjuvant across several cancer symptoms
items.content:
A single class can address multiple symptoms simultaneously near end of life.
Steroids slightly improve cancer pain compared to placebo and also improve fatigue, nausea, and well-being.
Dexamethasone (e.g., 8 mg in the morning) can benefit fatigue in some patients, usually toward the end of life, and high-dose steroids are commonly used for dyspnea from lymphangitic carcinomatosis, COPD, or asthma.
Long-term use is not well studied and patients should be observed for hyperglycemia.
keyPoints: Steroids modestly help pain plus fatigue, nausea, and well-being
Dexamethasone aids fatigue (often end-of-life) and dyspnea (lymphangitic carcinomatosis/COPD/asthma)
Watch for hyperglycemia; long-term safety not established
items.reference: NOCICEPTIVE PAIN: "Steroids did slightly improve pain compared to placebo in cancer patients and improved fatigue, nausea, and well-being..."

items.title: How to characterize cancer pain before treating it
items.content:
Effective analgesia begins with classification, not just a number.
At least 80% of cancer patients will experience pain during their lifetime, and although secondary to fatigue in prevalence, it is the most feared symptom.
Ask for a 0–10 rating for best, worst, and average pain and what an acceptable pain score would be, list what the patient has tried before (including acupuncture, massage, and other treatments), and ascertain whether the patient is on anticoagulants given widespread NOAC use.
Then characterize the pain into a treatable type (nociceptive, visceral, neuropathic, incident/bone) to direct therapy.
keyPoints: ≥80% of cancer patients have pain; it is the most feared symptom
Rate best/worst/average and acceptable score; review prior treatments; check anticoagulant use
Classify into nociceptive/visceral/neuropathic/incident to guide therapy
items.reference: PAIN (Frequency): "At least 80% of cancer patients will experience pain during their lifetime. While secondary to fatigue in prevalence, it is the most feared symptom."

items.title: How movement-related bone-metastasis pain is approached
items.content:
Incident (movement) pain from bone metastases is poorly served by evidence.
There is minimal evidence for any single modality to treat this type of pain.
In one small convenience trial, six of six patients with bone metastasis-incident pain had complete or near-complete responses to low-dose gabapentin (100–200 mg three times daily) added to opioids, though this has not been replicated.
There are also single case reports of benefit from Scrambler Therapy (cutaneous nerve stimulation) and 'burst' ketamine with morphine.
keyPoints: Incident bone pain has minimal high-quality evidence
Low-dose gabapentin (100–200 mg TID) added to opioids helped in a small unreplicated trial
Anecdotal options: Scrambler Therapy, burst ketamine with morphine
items.reference: INCIDENT (MOVEMENT) PAIN DUE TO BONE METASTASES: "six of six patients with bone metastasis-incident pain had complete or near-complete responses to low doses of gabapentin (100–200 mg three times a day) added to opioids, but this has not been repeated..."

items.title: Why taxanes and bevacizumab cause nasal vestibulitis and how to treat it
items.content:
An underrecognized chemotherapy toxicity affects the nasal vestibule.
Chemotherapy can damage the epithelial cells in the nose, leading to dryness, cracking, and secondary infection; like mucositis it generally heals in 7–10 days but is a potential source of infection.
More than 70–80% of patients receiving taxanes or bevacizumab experience untoward nasal symptoms such as pain, bleeding, and scabbing.
Nasal sprays of saline and of rose geranium in sesame oil have been used, with data supporting that the rose geranium preparation is more beneficial (it must be compounded).
keyPoints: Nasal vestibulitis: epithelial damage with dryness/cracking/infection, heals in ~7–10 days
>70–80% of taxane/bevacizumab patients get nasal symptoms (pain, bleeding, scabbing)
Rose geranium in sesame oil (compounded) outperforms saline spray
items.reference: NASAL VESTIBULITIS (Treatment): "Nasal sprays of saline and of rose geranium in sesame oil have been used to treat this clinical problem, with data supporting that the latter is more beneficial."

items.question: A randomized trial of weekly electronic patient-reported-outcome (PRO) symptom monitoring in metastatic cancer demonstrated which striking result?
options: No change in quality of life or survival
Improved quality of life and increased median overall survival (31.2 vs 26 months)
Improved survival but worse quality of life
items.explanation: Basch's trial of 766 metastatic solid-tumor patients showed weekly PRO monitoring with nurse follow-up improved quality of life and increased median overall survival to 31.2 months versus 26 months (p = .03).
items.reference: THE FIRST STEP: "median overall survival (OS) was 31.2 months in the PRO group and 26 months in the usual care group (p = .03)."

items.question: Compared with what patients spontaneously volunteer, using a structured 48-item symptom checklist revealed approximately how many symptoms?
items.explanation: Homsi et al. found the median number of volunteered symptoms was one, versus ten when a checklist was used — underscoring the value of systematic, structured assessment.
items.reference: THE FIRST STEP: "The median number of volunteered symptoms was one; the median number using a checklist was 10."

items.question: Which is the single most commonly reported symptom by cancer patients?
items.explanation: Fatigue is the most commonly reported symptom, often with an 80% or higher prevalence; pain is second in prevalence but the most feared symptom.
items.reference: FATIGUE (Frequency): "Fatigue is the most commonly reported symptom by patients, with often an 80% or higher prevalence."

items.question: A patient on pembrolizumab develops worsening fatigue. Beyond anemia and electrolyte issues, which immunotherapy-related cause must be specifically excluded — and may appear months after stopping the drug?
items.explanation: Checkpoint inhibitors cause hypothyroidism in 5–22% (and hypophysitis in 1–2%); while most hypothyroidism appears in 6–8 weeks, it can appear months after the immunotherapy has stopped.
items.reference: FATIGUE (Assessment): "while most cases of hypothyroidism manifest in 6–8 weeks, it can appear months after the patient has stopped the immunotherapy."

items.question: A patient is already on high-dose opioids for nociceptive cancer pain. What does the evidence say about adding acetaminophen?
options: It roughly doubles the analgesic effect
It is contraindicated due to bleeding risk
It eliminates the need for constipation prophylaxis
items.explanation: Recent studies show that adding acetaminophen to high doses of opioids has no additional effect. Opioids should still always be paired with constipation-preventing measures.
items.reference: NOCICEPTIVE PAIN: "Recent studies have shown that the addition of acetaminophen to high doses of opioids has no additional effect."

items.question: Which statement about opioids for neuropathic cancer pain is supported by randomized trials?
options: Opioids are ineffective for neuropathic pain
Opioids help neuropathic pain as much as gabapentin or nortriptyline, and combinations are more effective
items.explanation: Contrary to common teaching, randomized trials show opioids help neuropathic pain with as much benefit as gabapentin or nortriptyline, and the combination is even more effective.
items.reference: NEUROPATHIC PAIN: "randomized trials show that opioids do help neuropathic pain with as much benefit as gabapentin or nortriptyline; the combination is even more effective."

items.question: For established chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy after completion of neurotoxic chemotherapy, which agent has trial evidence of (modest) benefit?
items.explanation: Duloxetine can attenuate established CIPN symptoms to a small degree (0.6–1.0 on a 0–10 scale) and is currently the only proven treatment for established CIPN.
items.reference: CIPN: "duloxetine can attenuate established neuropathy symptoms to a small degree (0.6–1.0 on a 0–10 scale)."

items.question: What is currently the only clearly established way to prevent chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy?
options: Decreasing the dose or stopping the offending drug
items.explanation: There are no clearly established means for preventing CIPN other than decreasing the dose or stopping the offending drug; cryotherapy/compression are promising but investigational.
items.reference: CIPN: "there were no clearly established means for preventing CIPN from any agent, other than decreasing the dose or stopping the offending drug."

items.question: For a patient receiving bolus fluorouracil, which simple measure reduces the incidence and severity of oral mucositis by about 50%?
options: Sucking on ice chips during the infusion (oral cryotherapy)
items.explanation: For bolus fluorouracil, oral cryotherapy (sucking ice chips during the infusion) reduces mucositis incidence and severity by about 50%, by vasoconstriction limiting drug delivery to the oral mucosa.
items.reference: Treatment (Mucositis): "For patients receiving bolus fluorouracil, sucking on ice chips during infusion (oral cryotherapy) reduces the incidence and severity by ~50%."

items.question: What is the first-choice drug for chemotherapy-induced diarrhea from GI mucositis?
items.explanation: Loperamide is the first choice for GI mucositis diarrhea, up to 24 mg a day; octreotide up to 300 mg a day is reserved for refractory cases.
items.reference: Treatment (GI mucositis): "loperamide is the first choice, up to 24 mg a day. Octreotide up to 300 mg a day is reserved for refractory cases."

items.question: Which antiemetic, added to dexamethasone, a 5-HT3 antagonist, and an NK1 antagonist, was shown in the 2010s to be the most effective agent for chemotherapy-induced nausea and vomiting?
items.explanation: Olanzapine, added to the three established classes, further decreased chemotherapy-induced nausea/vomiting and appears to be the most effective of the agents discussed.
items.reference: Treatment (Chemotherapy N/V): "Olanzapine appears to be the most effective of all of the drugs mentioned in this section..."

items.question: Why were NK1 receptor antagonists added to, rather than substituted for, 5-HT3 receptor antagonists in antiemetic regimens?
options: NK1 antagonists did not reduce nausea/vomiting in the first 24 hours as 5-HT3 antagonists did
NK1 antagonists only work for non-chemotherapy nausea
items.explanation: NK1 receptor antagonists did not decrease nausea and vomiting in the first 24 hours (which 5-HT3 antagonists do), so they were added to the cocktail rather than replacing 5-HT3 antagonists.
items.reference: Treatment (Chemotherapy N/V): "These agents did not decrease nausea and vomiting in the first 24 h, as did the 5-HT3 receptor antagonists; thus, NK1 receptor antagonists were added to the antiemetic cocktail as opposed to replacing 5-HT3 receptor antagonists."

items.question: Which agent is recommended as first-line for cancer-related anorexia, with good evidence of benefit and without potentially serious harm?
items.explanation: Olanzapine is recommended as first-line for anorexia and the only agent with good evidence of benefit without serious harm (≥5% weight gain in 60% vs 9% placebo). Mirtazapine was ineffective and should not be used.
items.reference: ANOREXIA AND CACHEXIA (Treatment): "For anorexia, olanzapine is recommended as the first-line agent and the only agent with good evidence of benefit without potentially serious harm."

items.question: Which statement about managing cancer cachexia is correct?
options: Routine enteral feeding tubes and IV nutrition reverse cachexia
Cachexia generally cannot be reversed by simply replacing calories and nutrients
Mirtazapine is the most effective treatment
items.explanation: Cancer cachexia is a multifactorial metabolic syndrome that generally cannot be reversed by simply replacing calories; routine enteral feeding tubes and IV nutrition are not recommended.
items.reference: ANOREXIA AND CACHEXIA (Etiology): "In general, cancer cachexia cannot be reversed by simply replacing calories and nutrients."

items.question: Which is the recommended first-choice laxative for opioid-associated constipation in cancer patients?
items.explanation: Senna is the first choice (1–2 tablets up to 8) with adequate hydration; polyethylene glycol is added next, and peripherally acting opioid antagonists are used for refractory opioid-induced constipation.
items.reference: CONSTIPATION (Treatment): "Senna is the first choice, starting at 1–2 tablets a day and working up to 8, along with sufficient hydration."

items.question: Which is a first-line pharmacologic measure to relieve the sensation of air hunger in advanced cancer?
options: High-dose inhaled bronchodilators in all patients
items.explanation: Low-dose opioids are generally safe and helpful for relieving air hunger; low-dose benzodiazepines help associated anxiety, and a fan blowing cool air across the face can give comfort. Oxygen is for hypoxemia (SpO2 ≤90%).
items.reference: DYSPNEA (Treatment): "Opioids at low doses are generally safe and can be helpful in relieving air hunger..."

items.question: Supplemental oxygen for dyspnea is specifically indicated for patients with which finding?
options: Any subjective breathlessness regardless of saturation
items.explanation: Supplemental oxygen should be available for patients with hypoxemia (SpO2 ≤90% on room air); breathlessness without hypoxemia is better managed with opioids, benzodiazepines, and a fan.
items.reference: DYSPNEA (Treatment): "Supplemental oxygen should be available for patients with hypoxemia (i.e., SpO2 ≤ 90% on room air)."

items.question: A man on androgen deprivation for prostate cancer has bothersome hot flashes. Which has randomized-trial support in men?
options: Venlafaxine, which showed significant benefit in men
items.explanation: A randomized, placebo-controlled trial showed gabapentin 900 mg/d works well for hot flashes in men; a randomized trial of venlafaxine did not demonstrate significant benefit in men.
items.reference: MALES (Hot Flashes): "A prospective, randomized, placebo-controlled trial demonstrated that 900 mg/d of gabapentin works well for treating hot flashes in men..."

items.question: Fezolinetant, which became available in 2023 as a nonhormonal option for hot flashes, acts as which kind of agent?
items.explanation: Fezolinetant is an NK3 receptor antagonist; in studies including breast-cancer survivors, hot-flash reductions were similar whether or not patients were on tamoxifen.
items.reference: Treatment FEMALES (Hot Flashes): "Fezolinetant, an NK3 receptor antagonist, became clinically available in 2023 as a nonhormonal agent for treating hot flashes..."

items.question: Which chemotherapy agents are most associated with nasal vestibulitis (nasal pain, bleeding, scabbing)?
items.explanation: More than 70–80% of patients receiving taxanes or bevacizumab develop nasal symptoms; rose geranium in sesame oil (compounded) is more beneficial than saline spray.
items.reference: NASAL VESTIBULITIS (Frequency): ">70% of those receiving taxanes and >80% of those receiving bevacizumab experienced untoward nasal symptoms, such as pain, bleeding, and/or scabbing."

items.statement: Weekly patient-reported-outcome symptom monitoring in metastatic cancer improved only quality of life, with no effect on survival.
items.explanation: False — PRO monitoring improved quality of life AND median overall survival (31.2 vs 26 months, p=.03).
items.reference: THE FIRST STEP: "median overall survival (OS) was 31.2 months in the PRO group and 26 months in the usual care group (p = .03)."

items.statement: Pain is the most prevalent symptom in cancer patients, exceeding fatigue.
items.explanation: False — fatigue is the most common symptom (≥80%); pain is second in prevalence (≥80% lifetime) but is the most feared symptom.
items.reference: PAIN (Frequency): "While secondary to fatigue in prevalence, it is the most feared symptom."

items.statement: Whenever opioids are prescribed for cancer pain, constipation-preventing measures should always accompany them.
items.explanation: True — opioids should always be used with constipation-preventing measures, and every opioid prescription should include a plan to prevent constipation.
items.reference: NOCICEPTIVE PAIN: "then opioids, which should always be used with constipation-preventing measures."

items.statement: If a neuropathic pain drug is going to work, relief typically appears within 4–8 days, so trials need not exceed 2–3 weeks.
items.explanation: True — pain relief occurred in 4–8 days if it occurred, so trials of neuropathic pain drugs do not need to exceed 2–3 weeks.
items.reference: NEUROPATHIC PAIN: "Pain relief occurred in 4–8 days, if it occurred, so trials do not need to be >2–3 weeks."

items.statement: Among commonly used chemotherapy drugs, doxorubicin is the most common cause of oral mucositis.
items.explanation: True — although fluorouracil is the worst offender, it is rarely used now; among commonly used chemotherapy drugs, doxorubicin is the most common cause of oral mucositis.
items.reference: GASTROINTESTINAL AND ORAL MUCOSITIS (Frequency): "The most common cause of oral mucositis among commonly used chemotherapy drugs is doxorubicin."

items.statement: Most treatment-related mucositis develops about 7–10 days after treatment.
items.explanation: True — most mucositis happens 7–10 days after treatment; a different time course should prompt consideration of other causes (e.g., infection, laxatives).
items.reference: Etiology (Mucositis): "Most mucositis happens 7–10 days after treatment."

items.statement: All chemotherapy agents cause substantial nausea and vomiting to a similar degree.
items.explanation: False — emetogenicity is agent-specific: cisplatin and doxorubicin cause substantial nausea/vomiting, whereas fluorouracil does not cause substantial nausea/vomiting.
items.reference: NAUSEA AND VOMITING RELATED TO CHEMOTHERAPY: "Some agents, such as cisplatin and doxorubicin, cause substantial nausea and vomiting, whereas other agents, such as fluorouracil, do not cause any substantial nausea and vomiting."

items.statement: Mirtazapine is an effective, evidence-based treatment for cancer-related anorexia.
items.explanation: False — mirtazapine, although commonly prescribed, was ineffective in a large randomized trial and should not be used; olanzapine is the recommended first-line agent.
items.reference: ANOREXIA AND CACHEXIA (Treatment): "Mirtazapine, which is commonly prescribed, was ineffective in a large randomized trial and should not be used."

items.statement: Megestrol acetate and corticosteroids increase appetite and cause some weight gain but improve patient survival.
items.explanation: False — megestrol acetate and corticosteroids do increase appetite and cause some weight gain but have untoward side effects and do not appear to improve survival.
items.reference: ANOREXIA AND CACHEXIA (Treatment): "Megestrol acetate and corticosteroids do increase appetite...but have untoward side effects and do not appear to improve patient survival."

items.statement: Dyspnea in advanced cancer portends a poor prognosis and should trigger serious-illness conversations and advance-directive creation.
items.explanation: True — dyspnea portends a poor prognosis (survival of days, weeks, or months) and should trigger serious-illness conversations including being truthful about prognosis and creating an advance directive.
items.reference: DYSPNEA (Frequency): "Dyspnea portends a poor prognosis in advanced cancer with an average survival of only days, weeks, or months, and should trigger serious illness conversations..."

items.statement: Checkpoint-inhibitor pneumonitis can develop months after the immunotherapy has been stopped.
items.explanation: True — when reviewing dyspnea, remember that pneumonitis may develop months after the immunotherapy has stopped.
items.reference: DYSPNEA (Assessment): "remembering that pneumonitis may develop months after the immunotherapy has stopped."

items.statement: Hormone-deprivation hot flashes after cancer treatment reliably resolve within a couple of years.
items.explanation: False — although hot flashes were once thought to last only a couple of years, it is now understood that patients can have them for decades.
items.reference: HOT FLASHES (Frequency): "it is now understood that patients can have them for decades."

items.assertion: Clinicians should use a structured symptom scale rather than relying on what patients spontaneously report.
items.reason: Patients volunteer a median of about one symptom but report about ten when a structured checklist is used.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: because open questioning grossly under-captures symptoms (1 vs 10), structured PRO scales are recommended.
items.reference: THE FIRST STEP: "The median number of volunteered symptoms was one; the median number using a checklist was 10."

items.assertion: A patient who develops fatigue and hypothyroidism months after stopping a checkpoint inhibitor may still have an immunotherapy-related cause.
items.reason: Checkpoint-inhibitor hypothyroidism, though usually appearing in 6–8 weeks, can manifest months after the drug is stopped.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: delayed-onset checkpoint-inhibitor hypothyroidism means the immunotherapy can still be responsible months later.
items.reference: FATIGUE (Assessment): "while most cases of hypothyroidism manifest in 6–8 weeks, it can appear months after the patient has stopped the immunotherapy."

items.assertion: Opioids should be withheld from patients with neuropathic cancer pain because they are ineffective for it.
items.reason: Randomized trials show opioids relieve neuropathic pain as much as gabapentin or nortriptyline.
items.explanation: The assertion is false — opioids do help neuropathic pain (and combinations are better). The reason is true and contradicts the assertion. False assertion + true reason = option 3.
items.reference: NEUROPATHIC PAIN: "randomized trials show that opioids do help neuropathic pain with as much benefit as gabapentin or nortriptyline..."

items.assertion: Patients receiving bolus fluorouracil should suck on ice chips during the infusion.
items.reason: Oral cryotherapy reduces the incidence and severity of fluorouracil-induced oral mucositis by about 50%.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: oral cryotherapy halves mucositis from bolus 5-FU, so ice chips during infusion are recommended.
items.reference: Treatment (Mucositis): "sucking on ice chips during infusion (oral cryotherapy) reduces the incidence and severity by ~50%."

items.assertion: NK1 receptor antagonists replaced 5-HT3 receptor antagonists in the antiemetic cocktail.
items.reason: NK1 antagonists, unlike 5-HT3 antagonists, do not reduce nausea and vomiting in the first 24 hours.
items.explanation: The assertion is false — NK1 antagonists were ADDED to, not substituted for, 5-HT3 antagonists. The reason is true and explains why (they lack first-24-hour efficacy). False assertion + true reason = option 3.
items.reference: Treatment (Chemotherapy N/V): "NK1 receptor antagonists were added to the antiemetic cocktail as opposed to replacing 5-HT3 receptor antagonists."

items.assertion: Routine intravenous nutrition should be used to reverse cancer cachexia.
items.reason: Cancer cachexia is simply a deficit of calories and nutrients that resolves with adequate intake.
items.explanation: Both statements are false. Cachexia is a multifactorial metabolic syndrome that generally cannot be reversed by replacing calories, and enteral/IV nutrition is not recommended routinely. Both false = option 4.
items.reference: ANOREXIA AND CACHEXIA: "In general, cancer cachexia cannot be reversed by simply replacing calories and nutrients...Enteral feeding tubes and intravenous nutrition are not recommended and should not be used routinely."

items.assertion: A bowel regimen should be prescribed proactively with every opioid prescription.
items.reason: Opioids are a major cause of constipation, which occurs in 20–90% of cancer patients.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: because opioids commonly cause constipation, every opioid prescription should be paired with a prevention plan.
items.reference: CONSTIPATION (Treatment): "Every opioid prescription should be accompanied by a plan to prevent constipation."

items.assertion: Low-dose opioids can be used to relieve air hunger in advanced cancer.
items.reason: At low doses, opioids are generally safe in this setting and help relieve the sensation of breathlessness.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: low-dose opioids are generally safe and relieve air hunger, supporting their use for cancer dyspnea.
items.reference: DYSPNEA (Treatment): "Opioids at low doses are generally safe and can be helpful in relieving air hunger..."

items.assertion: Nonhormonal antidepressants are reasonable first options for hot flashes in women with breast cancer.
items.reason: Randomized trials show antidepressants such as venlafaxine and paroxetine substantially reduce hot flashes.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: because trials show antidepressants reduce hot flashes, they are appropriate nonhormonal options for breast-cancer patients.
items.reference: Treatment FEMALES (Hot Flashes): "randomized, placebo-controlled clinical trials that showed that these and other antidepressants substantially helped women with hot flashes."

items.assertion: It is useful to gauge how much each symptom actually bothers the patient rather than just recording its presence.
items.reason: Some symptoms, such as anorexia, may distress the family more than the patient.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: because a symptom like anorexia may bother the family but not the patient, assessing the degree of bother helps prioritize treatment.
items.reference: THE FIRST STEP: "It may be helpful to categorize how much each symptom actually bothers the patient; for instance, anorexia may bother the family but not the patient."

items.subtopic: Symptom Control in Patients with Cancer | THE FIRST STEP: IMPORTANCE OF PROPER SYMPTOM ASSESSMENT AND RESOLUTION
items.title: Prompt Symptom Assessment Improves Survival in Metastatic Cancer Patients
items.content: Regular monitoring and management of symptoms using patient-reported outcomes (PRO) significantly improve survival rates in metastatic cancer patients. Studies show that structured symptom assessment leads to better quality of life and increased median overall survival.
keyPoints: Randomized trials indicate prompt symptom assessment increases survival.
Median OS was 31.2 months with PRO monitoring vs. 26 months with usual care (p = .03).
Absolute difference in OS at 5 years: 6 per 100 patients.
items.reference: Symptom Control in Patients with Cancer · Symptom Control in Patients with Cancer | THE FIRST STEP: IMPORTANCE OF PROPER SYMPTOM ASSESSMENT AND RESOLUTION

items.subtopic: Symptom Control in Patients with Cancer | THE FIRST STEP: IMPORTANCE OF PROPER SYMPTOM ASSESSMENT AND RESOLUTION
items.title: Use of Patient-Reported Outcomes (PRO) Enhances Symptom Management and Quality of Life
items.content: Implementing a structured approach to symptom assessment using PRO scales can significantly improve the quality of life for cancer patients. This method allows for early detection and management of symptoms, leading to better patient outcomes.
keyPoints: Quality of life was significantly better in the PRO group compared to usual care.
PROs completed weekly led to significant improvements in quality of life at 3 months.
Fatigue and pain were the most common reported symptoms.
items.reference: Symptom Control in Patients with Cancer · Symptom Control in Patients with Cancer | THE FIRST STEP: IMPORTANCE OF PROPER SYMPTOM ASSESSMENT AND RESOLUTION

items.subtopic: Symptom Control in Patients with Cancer | THE FIRST STEP: IMPORTANCE OF PROPER SYMPTOM ASSESSMENT AND RESOLUTION
items.title: Accessibility of Symptom Assessment Tools for Effective Cancer Care
items.content: Even without advanced electronic systems, simple tools like the Edmonton Symptom Assessment System–Revised can be effectively used to assess symptoms in cancer patients. These tools are quick and easy to integrate into clinical practice.
keyPoints: Edmonton Symptom Assessment System–Revised is available on paper or tablets.
Includes questions on spirituality and financial distress, available in many languages.
Average completion time with a nurse's help: 117 seconds.
items.reference: Symptom Control in Patients with Cancer · Symptom Control in Patients with Cancer | THE FIRST STEP: IMPORTANCE OF PROPER SYMPTOM ASSESSMENT AND RESOLUTION

items.title: Understanding Fatigue in Cancer Patients: Etiology and Initial Assessment
items.content: Fatigue is a prevalent symptom among cancer patients, with an 80% or higher prevalence. It can result from the cancer itself, electrolyte imbalances like hypercalcemia, anemia, hypothyroidism, and treatments such as chemotherapy and immunotherapy (e.g., pembrolizumab, nivolumab, ipilimumab). Assessment should include checking for hypothyroidism, anemia, hypercalcemia, renal failure, depression, or demoralization. Notably, hypothyroidism can manifest 6–8 weeks after starting immunotherapy but may appear months later.
keyPoints: Fatigue prevalence in cancer patients: often 80% or higher
Etiology includes cancer, hypercalcemia, anemia, hypothyroidism, chemotherapy, and immunotherapy
Immunotherapy-related hypothyroidism incidence: 5-22% (average 6%)
De novo hypophysitis from immunotherapy: 1-2%
Hypothyroidism can manifest 6–8 weeks after starting immunotherapy but may appear months later
items.reference: Symptom Control in Patients with Cancer · FATIGUE

items.title: Effective Management Strategies for Cancer-Related Fatigue
items.content: Management of cancer-related fatigue involves correcting underlying causes such as anemia, hypothyroidism, and electrolyte abnormalities. Structured exercise like daily walking can be both preventative and therapeutic. Medications such as methylphenidate (5 mg at 7 and 11 A.M.) for short-term trials, modafinil, or bupropion may help; bupropion has shown significant reductions in fatigue with minimal side effects. Steroids like dexamethasone (8 mg) can be beneficial, particularly towards the end of life, though long-term use requires monitoring for hyperglycemia.
keyPoints: Correct underlying causes: anemia, hypothyroidism, electrolyte abnormalities
Structured exercise: 45 min daily walking
Methylphenidate trial: 5 mg at 7 and 11 A.M. for 48 h
Bupropion benefits: significant fatigue reduction with minimal side effects
Steroids (e.g., dexamethasone 8 mg) may benefit, especially end-of-life; monitor for hyperglycemia
items.reference: Symptom Control in Patients with Cancer · FATIGUE

items.title: Approach to Cancer Pain Management
items.content: Cancer pain is prevalent in at least 80% of patients and requires a structured approach for assessment and treatment. Begin with assessing the type of pain (nociceptive, visceral, neuropathic) and use appropriate pharmacological strategies.
keyPoints: At least 80% of cancer patients will experience pain during their lifetime.
For nociceptive pain: Start with acetaminophen, then NSAIDs, followed by opioids; add constipation-preventing measures.
Steroids improve pain (reduction of 0.84 on a 0-10 scale at 1 week) and other symptoms like fatigue, nausea, well-being.
items.reference: Symptom Control in Patients with Cancer · PAIN

items.title: Management of Visceral Pain in Cancer Patients
items.content: Visceral pain is challenging to treat. Use a combination of NSAIDs or neuropathic medications with opioids. Consider octreotide for malignant bowel obstruction if other treatments fail.
keyPoints: Visceral pain is among the hardest to treat.
Combine NSAIDs or neuropathic agents like gabapentin/pregabalin with opioids.
Octreotide may reduce visceral hyperalgesia in cases of malignant bowel obstruction.
items.reference: Symptom Control in Patients with Cancer · PAIN

items.title: Treatment Strategies for Chemotherapy-Induced Peripheral Neuropathy (CIPN)
items.content: CIPN is a significant issue with certain chemotherapeutic agents. Prevention strategies include dose reduction or cryotherapy/compression therapy during treatment. Duloxetine may slightly alleviate established CIPN symptoms.
keyPoints: Chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy affects most patients receiving chemotherapy.
Preventive measures: Dose reduction, cryotherapy, compression therapy (ongoing trial in 2024).
For established CIPN, duloxetine can reduce symptoms by 0.6–1.0 on a 0-10 scale.
items.reference: Symptom Control in Patients with Cancer · PAIN

items.subtopic: GASTROINTESTINAL AND ORAL MUCOSITIS RELATED TO TREATMENT
items.title: Oral Mucositis Incidence and Prevention in Cancer Treatment
items.content: Oral mucositis is a common side effect of certain cancer treatments, particularly molecularly targeted agents. The incidence varies by drug, with 30–40% of patients on regorafenib, sorafenib, erdafitinib, or palbociclib experiencing it. Oral cryotherapy during bolus fluorouracil administration can reduce the severity and incidence by approximately 50%. There is conflicting evidence regarding the efficacy of glucocorticoid rinses for prevention.
keyPoints: Oral mucositis occurs in 30–40% of patients on certain molecularly targeted agents.
Bolus fluorouracil-associated oral cryotherapy reduces incidence and severity by ~50%.
Conflicting evidence exists regarding the use of glucocorticoid rinses for prevention.
items.reference: Symptom Control in Patients with Cancer · GASTROINTESTINAL AND ORAL MUCOSITIS RELATED TO TREATMENT

items.subtopic: GASTROINTESTINAL AND ORAL MUCOSITIS RELATED TO TREATMENT
items.title: Management of Chemotherapy-Induced Gastrointestinal Mucositis
items.content: Chemotherapy-induced GI mucositis can lead to significant diarrhea, affecting 50–80% of cancer patients. It typically occurs 7–10 days post-treatment and is managed with loperamide up to 24 mg/day. For refractory cases, octreotide up to 300 mg/day may be used. Glucocorticoids are also commonly employed in treatment.
keyPoints: GI mucositis affects 50–80% of cancer patients undergoing chemotherapy.
Diarrhea typically occurs 7–10 days after treatment initiation.
Loperamide is the first-line treatment, up to 24 mg/day.
Octreotide may be used for refractory cases, up to 300 mg/day.
items.reference: Symptom Control in Patients with Cancer · GASTROINTESTINAL AND ORAL MUCOSITIS RELATED TO TREATMENT

items.subtopic: NAUSEA AND VOMITING RELATED TO CHEMOTHERAPY
items.title: Understanding Chemotherapy-Induced Nausea and Vomiting: Etiology and Assessment
items.content: Chemotherapy-induced nausea and vomiting (CINV) are common side effects, with the severity largely dependent on the specific chemotherapy agents used. Agents like cisplatin and doxorubicin are known to cause significant CINV, whereas others such as fluorouracil typically do not. Clinicians assess CINV by patient interviews or family reports, often during follow-up visits for subsequent therapy cycles. PRO data from clinical trials can also inform assessment.
keyPoints: Cisplatin and doxorubicin cause substantial nausea and vomiting.
Fluorouracil does not cause substantial nausea and vomiting.
Assessment involves patient/family interviews and PRO data.
items.reference: Symptom Control in Patients with Cancer · NAUSEA AND VOMITING RELATED TO CHEMOTHERAPY

items.subtopic: NAUSEA AND VOMITING RELATED TO CHEMOTHERAPY
items.title: Optimizing Antiemetic Therapy for Highly Emetogenic Chemotherapy
items.content: For highly emetogenic chemotherapy, antiemetic regimens are initiated at the start of treatment and continued for several days. Dexamethasone has been a cornerstone since the 1970s, with 5-HT3 receptor antagonists added in the 1990s. NK1 receptor antagonists were introduced in the 2000s to address delayed nausea and vomiting beyond the first 24 hours. Olanzapine, established in the 2010s, has shown superior efficacy when combined with these agents, suggesting potential reductions in dexamethasone use.
keyPoints: Dexamethasone is a key antiemetic since the 1970s.
5-HT3 receptor antagonists were added in the 1990s.
NK1 receptor antagonists address delayed nausea and vomiting.
Olanzapine, effective when combined with other agents, may reduce reliance on dexamethasone.
items.reference: Symptom Control in Patients with Cancer · NAUSEA AND VOMITING RELATED TO CHEMOTHERAPY

items.subtopic: NAUSEA AND VOMITING UNRELATED TO CHEMOTHERAPY | ANOREXIA AND CACHEXIA
items.title: Olanzapine for Nausea and Anorexia in Advanced Cancer Patients
items.content: Olanzapine has been shown to be effective in reducing nausea and improving weight gain in advanced cancer patients not receiving chemotherapy or radiation therapy. A 2020 trial demonstrated significant reduction in nausea scores with olanzapine compared to placebo, while a subsequent study confirmed its efficacy for anorexia.
keyPoints: Olanzapine (5 mg/d) significantly reduced nausea from 9/10 to 2/10 (p < .001).
In a trial of 30 patients, olanzapine was effective in reducing nausea and vomiting.
For anorexia, olanzapine led to weight gain in 60% of patients versus 9% with placebo (p < .001) in a trial involving 124 solid tumor patients.
items.reference: Symptom Control in Patients with Cancer · NAUSEA AND VOMITING UNRELATED TO CHEMOTHERAPY | ANOREXIA AND CACHEXIA

items.subtopic: NAUSEA AND VOMITING UNRELATED TO CHEMOTHERAPY | ANOREXIA AND CACHEXIA
items.title: Etiology and Assessment of Nausea and Anorexia in Cancer Patients
items.content: Nausea and vomiting in cancer patients not related to chemotherapy can result from various causes including radiation therapy, bowel obstruction, and cerebral metastases. Anorexia is often caused by hormonal release from tumors but can be exacerbated by treatments and other factors.
keyPoints: Etiologies for nausea include radiation, bowel obstruction, medications, electrolyte abnormalities, post-anesthesia, and cerebral metastases.
Anorexia in cancer patients affects 5–25% of community-dwelling adults and up to twice that number with cancer.
Cachexia is defined by weight loss of muscle and adipose tissue, metabolic imbalance, and reduced food intake.
items.reference: Symptom Control in Patients with Cancer · NAUSEA AND VOMITING UNRELATED TO CHEMOTHERAPY | ANOREXIA AND CACHEXIA

items.subtopic: NAUSEA AND VOMITING UNRELATED TO CHEMOTHERAPY | ANOREXIA AND CACHEXIA
items.title: Treatment Considerations for Cancer-Related Anorexia and Cachexia
items.content: Olanzapine is recommended as the first-line treatment for anorexia in cancer patients due to its efficacy without serious harm. Other treatments like mirtazapine are ineffective, while megestrol acetate and corticosteroids have side effects and do not improve survival.
keyPoints: Olanzapine is recommended as the first-line treatment for anorexia in cancer patients.
Mirtazapine was found ineffective in a large trial and should not be used.
Megestrol acetate and corticosteroids increase appetite but have side effects and do not improve survival.
items.reference: Symptom Control in Patients with Cancer · NAUSEA AND VOMITING UNRELATED TO CHEMOTHERAPY | ANOREXIA AND CACHEXIA

items.title: Prevention and Management of Constipation in Cancer Patients
items.content: Constipation is prevalent among cancer patients due to various medications and lifestyle factors. Prevention should start with good bowel hygiene, including the use of stool softeners like senna alongside adequate hydration.
keyPoints: Frequency: 20–90% of all cancer patients experience constipation at some point.
Etiology: Opioids, chemotherapy agents (e.g., vandetanib), and lifestyle factors contribute to constipation.
Assessment: Constipation is likely if the patient has not had a bowel movement in 3 or more days.
Treatment: Start with senna (1–2 tablets daily) and hydration; consider polyethylene glycol if needed. Opioid antagonists may be used for refractory cases.
items.reference: Symptom Control in Patients with Cancer · CONSTIPATION | DYSPNEA

items.title: Assessment and Management of Dyspnea in Advanced Cancer Patients
items.content: Dyspnea is a common symptom in advanced cancer, indicating poor prognosis. It requires systematic assessment to identify reversible causes and appropriate management strategies.
keyPoints: Frequency: 10–70% of patients with advanced cancer report dyspnea.
Etiology: Multifactorial; includes lung dysfunction from cancer or treatments like immunotherapy (incidence 9.5%).
Assessment: Identify reversible causes such as hypoxia or pneumonia; review medication history for checkpoint inhibitors.
Treatment: Address underlying causes, use high-dose steroids for specific conditions, and consider low-dose opioids and benzodiazepines for symptom relief.
items.reference: Symptom Control in Patients with Cancer · CONSTIPATION | DYSPNEA

items.title: Use of Supplemental Oxygen in Dyspnea Management
items.content: Supplemental oxygen is crucial for managing hypoxemia in cancer patients experiencing dyspnea, with specific criteria guiding its use.
keyPoints: Indication: Use supplemental oxygen if $ SpO_2 \<= 90	ext{%} $ on room air.
Consideration: High-flow nasal oxygen (HFNO) can be a bridge therapy but complicates hospice or home care due to cost.
items.reference: Symptom Control in Patients with Cancer · CONSTIPATION | DYSPNEA

items.title: Management of Hot Flashes in Cancer Patients
items.content: Hot flashes are a common symptom experienced by cancer patients undergoing hormone therapy. In postmenopausal women treated for breast cancer, about two-thirds experience hot flashes, and almost half have night sweats. Similarly, three-quarters of men treated with orchiectomy for prostate cancer will have hot flashes. The underlying cause is the rapid decrease in estrogen or testosterone levels.
keyPoints: Hot flashes occur in ~66% of postmenopausal women treated for breast cancer; ~50% experience night sweats.
Approximately 75% of men undergoing orchiectomy for prostate cancer experience hot flashes.
Etiology: Rapid decrease in estrogen (women) or testosterone (men).
Antidepressants like venlafaxine, paroxetine, sertraline, and fluoxetine are effective treatments for women; citalopram 20 mg/d is recommended due to efficacy and tolerability.
Gabapentin reduces hot flashes and anxiety in breast cancer survivors; 900 mg/d is effective for men with prostate cancer.
Oxybutynin (immediate or sustained release) is effective for both sexes.
Megestrol acetate 20-40 mg/day can control refractory hot flashes in women.
Fezolinetant, an NK3 receptor antagonist, is a nonhormonal option available since 2023 with similar efficacy to other treatments.
Progesterone analogues decrease hot flashes in men similarly to their effect in women.
items.reference: Symptom Control in Patients with Cancer · HOT FLASHES

items.title: Duration and Assessment of Hot Flashes in Cancer Patients
items.content: Hot flashes can persist for decades, contrary to the previous belief that they lasted only a couple of years. It is important to assess hot flashes by asking about their frequency, severity, and impact on life quality as part of appropriate care for prostate or breast cancer patients.
keyPoints: Hot flashes may last for decades in cancer patients undergoing hormone therapy.
Assessment should include questions about frequency, severity, and interference with daily life.
items.reference: Symptom Control in Patients with Cancer · HOT FLASHES

items.title: High Incidence of Nasal Symptoms in Patients Receiving Specific Chemotherapies
items.content: Patients undergoing chemotherapy with taxanes, bevacizumab, paclitaxel, or nab-paclitaxel have a high likelihood (>70% for taxanes and >80% for bevacizumab) of experiencing nasal symptoms such as pain, bleeding, and scabbing. These symptoms arise due to chemotherapy-induced damage to the epithelial cells in the nose.
keyPoints: >70% of patients on taxanes experience nasal symptoms
>80% of patients on bevacizumab experience nasal symptoms
>75% of patients on paclitaxel, nab-paclitaxel, or bevacizumab develop bothersome nasal symptoms
Symptoms generally heal in 7–10 days
items.reference: Symptom Control in Patients with Cancer · NASAL VESTIBULITIS

items.title: Effective Treatment for Chemotherapy-Induced Nasal Vestibulitis
items.content: Treatment of chemotherapy-induced nasal vestibulitis can be effectively managed with compounded nasal sprays. Rose geranium in sesame oil has been shown to be more beneficial than saline sprays.
keyPoints: Rose geranium in sesame oil is more effective than saline for treating nasal symptoms
Nasal sprays need to be compounded as per available recipes from the authors or Mayo Clinic
items.reference: Symptom Control in Patients with Cancer · NASAL VESTIBULITIS

items.question: Which medication is mentioned as having significant reductions in fatigue with minimal side effects according to a systematic review?
items.explanation: The passage mentions that bupropion has significant reductions in fatigue with minimal side effects according to a systematic review.

items.question: What is the most common symptom experienced by cancer patients during their lifetime?
items.explanation: At least 80% of cancer patients will experience pain during their lifetime, making it the most common and feared symptom.

items.question: Which medication is recommended as a first-line treatment for nociceptive pain in cancer patients?
items.explanation: For nociceptive pain, the recommended first-line treatment is acetaminophen.

items.question: What is a promising approach to decrease the concentration of paclitaxel in peripheral extremities?
options: Combination of cryotherapy and compression therapy

items.question: What is the effect of duloxetine on established chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy (CIPN) symptoms?
options: Small degree attenuation of neuropathy symptoms
items.explanation: Duloxetine can attenuate established neuropathy symptoms to a small degree (0.6–1.0 on a 0–10 scale).

items.question: Which chemotherapy drug is most commonly associated with causing oral mucositis among the drugs still frequently used?

items.question: What is a recommended treatment for reducing the incidence and severity of oral mucositis during bolus fluorouracil infusion?

Inquiring when the patient last had a bowel movement
Checking if it has been 3 or more days since the last bowel movement
